metadata.title: Comprehensive Coptic Lexicon - Sahidic Dialect
metadata.description: Systematic compilation from Crum Dictionary, Biblical corpus, Nag Hammadi, and literary sources

CR0001.notes: Primary word for God in Christian contexts

CR0002.notes: Used for both earthly and divine authority

CR0005.notes: Primary copula for masculine singular subjects

CR0006.notes: Primary copula for feminine singular subjects

CR0013.notes: General term for location or place

CR0016.notes: Can refer to children of either gender

NH0001.notes: Central Gnostic concept of spiritual knowledge

VB0001.notes: Most common Coptic verb for giving

DL0001.notes: Primary staple food in ancient Egypt, essential for daily sustenance. Often used metaphorically in biblical contexts for spiritual nourishment.

DL0002.notes: Essential element for life, frequently used in baptismal and purification contexts. Symbol of spiritual cleansing in Christian texts.

DL0003.notes: Fermented grape beverage, central to Eucharistic theology and daily life. Often contrasted with water in religious contexts.

DL0004.etymology: Egyptian jw.f (flesh, meat). From the root jw (meat) + -f (suffix). Archaeological evidence shows meat consumption was often limited to special occasions and religious festivals in ancient Egypt, making it a valuable commodity.
DL0004.notes: Denotes both physical meat as food and flesh in theological contexts. In Christian texts, often contrasted with spiritual matters. Monastic literature frequently discusses abstinence from meat as spiritual discipline.

DL0005.etymology: Egyptian jt (barley). Barley was one of the earliest domesticated grains in Egypt, dating to the Predynastic period. It was a crucial crop for both human consumption and beer production, which was a daily staple.
DL0005.notes: Essential grain crop in ancient Egypt, used for bread and beer production. In biblical contexts, often associated with simple, humble food offerings. Archaeological evidence shows barley was distributed as rations to workers.

DL0006.etymology: Egyptian mrḥ.t (oil, fat, ointment). From the root mrḥ (to anoint, oil). Oil production from olives, sesame, and other sources was crucial for cooking, lighting, and religious ceremonies in ancient Egypt.
DL0006.notes: Essential substance for cooking, lighting, and religious anointing. In Christian contexts, prominently featured in sacramental anointing and healing practices. Economic texts show oil was a valuable trade commodity.

DL0007.etymology: Egyptian pr (house, estate). From the hieroglyphic pr, literally 'that which goes out/comes forth', referring to the structure that emerges from the ground. The word encompasses both physical dwellings and estates.
DL0007.notes: Fundamental concept encompassing physical dwellings and metaphorical spiritual homes. In Christian texts, often refers to heavenly mansions and the Church as God's house. Legal papyri show complex property relationships.

DL0008.etymology: Egyptian ꜥꜣ (door, gateway). From the root ꜥꜣ meaning 'great, large', originally referring to the large entrance gates of temples and palaces. Later extended to any door or entrance.
DL0008.notes: Both literal architectural feature and powerful metaphor for spiritual access. In Christian symbolism, Christ as the door to salvation. Archaeological evidence shows elaborate door construction in temples and wealthy homes.

DL0009.etymology: Egyptian ḥnw (pot, vessel). From the root ḥnw meaning 'to contain/hold'. Pottery was essential for storage, cooking, and serving in ancient Egyptian households, with sophisticated ceramic traditions.
DL0009.notes: Essential household container for storage and cooking. Archaeological evidence shows sophisticated pottery traditions. In biblical narratives, often associated with miraculous provision and anointing.

DL0010.etymology: Greek λύχνος (lamp, light). Adopted into Coptic during the Ptolemaic period when Greek lighting technology influenced Egyptian practices. Oil lamps became widespread in Christian monasteries and churches.
DL0010.notes: Greek loanword for illumination devices, especially oil lamps. In Christian symbolism, represents spiritual enlightenment and Christ as light of the world. Monastic literature shows lamps were essential for nighttime prayers.

DL0011.etymology: Egyptian ḥbs (to clothe, garment). From the root ḥbs meaning 'to cover/clothe'. Clothing production and trade were major economic activities in ancient Egypt, with elaborate textile traditions.
DL0011.notes: Generic term for clothing and garments. In Christian contexts, often discussed in relation to modesty and spiritual priorities. Economic texts show clothing was a significant trade commodity and marker of social status.

DL0012.etymology: Egyptian tbw.t (sandal, shoe). From the root tbw (to tread/step). Footwear in ancient Egypt ranged from simple reed sandals for commoners to elaborate leather shoes for the elite.
DL0012.notes: Essential footwear for protection and status display. In biblical contexts, removing shoes signifies holy ground. Archaeological finds show sophisticated leatherworking and diverse sandal styles across social classes.

DL0013.etymology: Egyptian hrw (day, daytime). From the root hrw meaning 'day/light'. The concept of day as divine light was central to Egyptian cosmology, with the sun god Ra's daily journey across the sky.
DL0013.notes: Fundamental temporal concept in both practical and cosmological contexts. In Christian theology, often symbolizes spiritual enlightenment and the age of salvation. Calendar texts show sophisticated timekeeping systems.

DL0014.etymology: Egyptian grḥ (night, darkness). From the root grḥ meaning 'to be dark/end'. Night held profound significance in Egyptian cosmology as the time of the sun god's underworld journey and spiritual trials.
DL0014.notes: Cosmologically significant as time of spiritual trials and divine mysteries. In Christian contexts, associated with Christ's passion, spiritual warfare, and monastic night prayers. Egyptian astronomical texts show sophisticated understanding of nocturnal cycles.

DL0015.etymology: Egyptian bꜣk.w (work, labor, service). From the root bꜣk meaning 'to work/serve'. Work held divine significance in Egyptian culture, with creation itself viewed as cosmic labor and human work as participation in divine order.
DL0015.notes: Central concept encompassing physical labor, spiritual work, and divine service. In Christian theology, work is both practical necessity and spiritual discipline. Monastic literature emphasizes 'ora et labora' - prayer and work as unified spiritual practice.

DL0016.etymology: Egyptian mšꜥ (army, soldier). From the root mšꜥ meaning 'to march/expedition'. Egyptian military organization was sophisticated, with professional soldiers serving pharaoh and regional governors in defense and conquest.
DL0016.notes: Professional military class in ancient Egypt and later Christian contexts. In Christian literature, often used metaphorically for spiritual warfare and discipleship. Archaeological evidence shows detailed military organization and foreign mercenary service.

DL0017.etymology: Egyptian sš (scribe). From the root sš meaning 'to write'. Scribes held elite status in Egyptian society as keepers of knowledge, law, and divine texts. Their literacy made them indispensable to administration and religion.
DL0017.notes: Elite literate class fundamental to Egyptian civilization and early Christianity. In Christian contexts, often represents learned interpreters of Scripture. Archaeological evidence shows scribal schools and sophisticated educational systems.

DL0018.etymology: Egyptian wꜥb (priest, pure one). From the root wꜥb meaning 'to be pure/clean'. Priests maintained ritual purity essential for temple service and mediating between divine and human realms.
DL0018.notes: Sacred office requiring ritual purity and specialized knowledge. In Christian contexts, encompasses both Old Testament priesthood and Christian ministerial orders. Temple papyri show complex priestly hierarchies and ritual responsibilities.

DL0019.etymology: Egyptian ꜥḥwty (field worker, farmer). From the root ꜥḥ meaning 'field/cultivated land'. Agriculture formed the foundation of Egyptian civilization, with farmers providing sustenance for all social classes.
DL0019.notes: Fundamental occupation in Egyptian society, providing food security for civilization. In Christian parables, farming represents spiritual cultivation and divine harvest. Economic papyri show complex agricultural systems and land tenure.

DL0020.etymology: Greek τράπεζα (table, bank). From Greek tetra (four) + peza (foot), literally 'four-footed'. Adopted during Hellenistic period when Greek dining customs influenced Egyptian practices.
DL0020.notes: Greek loanword for dining furniture, reflecting Hellenistic influence on domestic life. In Christian contexts, prominently featured in Last Supper narratives and Eucharistic theology. Monastic literature shows tables as focal points of community meals.

DL0021.etymology: Egyptian tf.t (basket, container). From the root tf meaning 'to weave/plait'. Basket-making was a crucial craft in Egypt, using papyrus and palm leaves for storage, transport, and daily household needs.
DL0021.notes: Essential household container made from woven plant materials. In biblical narratives, frequently associated with miraculous provision and abundance. Archaeological evidence shows sophisticated basket-weaving techniques and diverse functional forms.

DL0022.etymology: Egyptian śnꜥ (knife, razor). From the root śnꜥ meaning 'to cut/slice'. Knives were essential tools for food preparation, crafts, and ritual purposes, made from copper, bronze, and later iron.
DL0022.notes: Essential cutting tool for daily life and ritual purposes. In biblical contexts, associated with sacrifice, violence, and division. Archaeological finds show sophisticated metallurgy and blade-making techniques across Egyptian periods.

DL0023.etymology: Egyptian rnp.t (year, regnal year). From the root rnp meaning 'to be young/renew'. Years were fundamental to Egyptian chronology, marked by Nile floods and royal regnal dating systems.
DL0023.notes: Fundamental chronological unit tied to agricultural cycles and royal dating. In Christian contexts, often marks prophetic periods and salvation history. Egyptian calendar systems show sophisticated astronomical knowledge.

DL0024.etymology: Egyptian ꜣbd (month). From the root ꜣbd meaning 'lunar cycle'. Egyptian months followed lunar observations combined with civil calendar adjustments for agricultural timing.
DL0024.notes: Lunar-based time division fundamental to Egyptian civil and religious calendars. In biblical contexts, marks ritual observances and prophetic periods. Astronomical texts show sophisticated lunar observation methods.

DL0025.etymology: Egyptian wnw.t (hour, time period). From the root wnw meaning 'time/period'. Egyptian time-keeping divided day and night into twelve hours each, varying seasonally with daylight length.
DL0025.notes: Precise time measurement crucial for astronomical observations and religious rituals. In Christian contexts, marks liturgical hours and eschatological periods. Egyptian sundials and water clocks show advanced time-keeping technology.

DL0026.etymology: Egyptian m-tn (at this time). From the preposition m (in/at) + tn (this). Temporal deixis expressing immediate present time. The construction parallels Demotic m-tn and reflects ancient Egyptian ways of conceptualizing present time.
DL0026.notes: Essential temporal deictic for expressing eschatological 'now' in Christian contexts. Paul's 'now is the acceptable time' (2 Cor 6:2) emphasizes present salvation opportunity. Philosophical texts use it for immediate present consciousness versus eternal time.

DL0027.etymology: Egyptian ḥtp (rest, peace, satisfaction). From the root ḥtp meaning 'to be satisfied/at peace'. The concept encompasses physical rest, spiritual peace, and divine satisfaction. Egyptian mortuary texts speak of the deceased finding ḥtp in the afterlife.
DL0027.notes: Jesus promises rest to the weary (Mt 11:28), representing spiritual peace and divine sabbath. Monastic literature emphasizes both physical rest and spiritual quietude. In Gnostic texts, represents cessation from worldly concerns and entry into divine contemplation.

DL0028.etymology: Greek μαθητής (learner, disciple). From μανθάνω (to learn). In Christian contexts, specifically refers to followers of Jesus who learn through both instruction and imitation. The term encompasses intellectual learning and spiritual formation.
DL0028.notes: Core Christian identity centered on learning from and following Jesus. The Twelve disciples represent the inner circle, but the term applies broadly to all Christian believers. In Gnostic literature, refers to those receiving secret knowledge. Monastic tradition emphasizes discipleship as lifelong spiritual formation.

DL0029.etymology: Egyptian sbꜣ (teach, instruct). From the root sbꜣ meaning 'to teach/educate'. Teaching was revered in Egyptian culture, with scribal schools training administrators and priests. The title carried both intellectual and moral authority.
DL0029.notes: Jesus as rabbi/teacher represents divine wisdom incarnate. Paul lists teachers among spiritual gifts (1 Cor 12:28). Monastic tradition emphasizes abbas as spiritual teachers guiding disciples. Egyptian scribal schools preserved this educational model in Christian contexts.

DL0030.etymology: Greek ἔμπορος (merchant, trader). From ἐμπορία (commerce, trade). Merchants were crucial to ancient Mediterranean economy, facilitating trade between Egypt, Asia, and Europe. Often traveled extensively and accumulated considerable wealth.
DL0030.notes: Jesus uses merchant seeking pearls as kingdom parable (Mt 13:45). Revelation depicts merchants mourning Babylon's fall (Rev 18), representing worldly commerce versus spiritual treasure. Egyptian merchants facilitated international trade through Alexandria and Red Sea ports.

DL0032.etymology: Greek χιτών (tunic, shirt). Basic undergarment worn next to skin in Greek and Roman fashion. Adopted into Coptic clothing vocabulary during Hellenistic period. Essential daily garment for both rich and poor.
DL0032.notes: Jesus' teaching about giving your tunic to one who takes your cloak (Mt 5:40). John 19:23 describes soldiers dividing Christ's garments including his tunic. Represents basic necessity and Christian generosity. Monastic literature discusses simple clothing as spiritual discipline.

DL0033.etymology: Egyptian dn.t (belt, girdle, waistband). From the root dn meaning 'to bind/encircle'. Belts served both practical and symbolic functions in ancient Egypt, indicating social status and profession. Warriors, priests, and officials wore distinctive belts.
DL0033.notes: John the Baptist's leather belt (Mt 3:4) symbolizes prophetic simplicity. Paul's 'belt of truth' (Eph 6:14) represents spiritual preparedness. Monastic traditions emphasize simple cord belts as symbols of poverty and discipline. Archaeological finds show elaborate royal and priestly belts.

DL0034.etymology: Egyptian ssn.t (linen, fine cloth). From the root ssn meaning 'to weave/spin'. Linen was Egypt's premium textile, made from flax grown in the Nile Delta. Egyptian linen was renowned throughout the ancient world for its quality and fineness.
DL0034.notes: Rich man clothed in purple and fine linen (Lk 16:19) represents luxury and wealth. Revelation describes righteous acts as 'fine linen' (Rev 19:8). Egyptian mummy wrappings used finest linen for preservation. Priestly garments required pure linen for ritual purity.

DL0035.etymology: Egyptian ḥnw.t (window, opening, gap). From the root ḥn meaning 'to open/reveal'. Windows in Egyptian architecture served both practical light/air functions and symbolic roles as portals between earthly and divine realms.
DL0035.notes: Noah opens ark window to release dove (Gn 8:6). Rahab lowers spies through window (Jos 2:15). Eutychus falls from window while Paul preaches (Acts 20:9). Symbolically represents divine revelation and spiritual vision breaking through earthly barriers.

DL0036.etymology: Egyptian tp (top, head, roof). From the root tp meaning 'head/top/upper part'. In Egyptian architecture, roofs had both practical and cosmic significance, representing the celestial vault and divine protection over earthly dwellings.
DL0036.notes: Paralytic lowered through roof to Jesus (Mk 2:4) shows determination to reach divine healing. Peter's rooftop vision (Acts 10:9) represents divine revelation from above. Roof represents boundary between earthly and heavenly realms, place of prayer and divine encounter.

DL0037.etymology: Egyptian nst (seat, throne, chair). From the root nst meaning 'seat/throne'. Chairs and seats held significant social and religious meaning in Egyptian culture, indicating status, authority, and divine presence. Pharaoh's throne represented cosmic order.
DL0037.notes: Moses' seat represents teaching authority (Mt 23:2). Twenty-four elders on thrones around God's throne (Rev 4:4) symbolize divine council. Egyptian chairs often had lion legs representing royal power. Simple stools for common people, elaborate thrones for rulers and deities.

DL0040.etymology: Greek καρπός (fruit, harvest, produce). Borrowed into Coptic during Hellenistic period when Greek agricultural and botanical terminology became widespread. The concept encompasses both literal fruit and metaphorical 'fruits' of labor or spiritual development.
DL0040.notes: Central metaphor in Christian teaching for spiritual productivity and moral behavior. 'Fruits of the Spirit' (Gal 5:22) represents Christian virtues. Agricultural contexts include literal crops and harvests. Parable of the barren fig tree emphasizes spiritual fruitfulness as divine expectation.

DL0041.etymology: Egyptian ḥmw (salt, natron). From the root ḥmw meaning 'salt/natron'. Salt was crucial in Egyptian mummification, food preservation, and ritual purification. Natron deposits from Wadi Natrun provided essential salts for religious and practical uses.
DL0041.notes: Symbol of covenant preservation and spiritual purity in biblical contexts. Jesus calls disciples 'salt of the earth' (Mt 5:13), emphasizing their preserving role in society. Egyptian natron salt was essential for mummification, representing eternal preservation and spiritual transformation.

DL0042.etymology: Egyptian šm.w (vegetables, herbs, greens). From the root šm meaning 'green plants/herbs'. Vegetables were essential to Egyptian diet, with elaborate garden cultivation along the Nile. Medical papyri document numerous medicinal properties of specific greens.
DL0042.notes: Daniel and friends eat vegetables instead of royal food (Dan 1:12), representing spiritual discipline and divine blessing. Paul mentions weak faith eating only vegetables (Rom 14:2). Egyptian gardens produced lettuce, onions, garlic, and herbs essential for both nutrition and medicine.

DL0043.etymology: Greek γάλα (milk). From Proto-Indo-European *ǵlakt- meaning 'milk'. Milk represented nourishment, maternal care, and divine provision. In Egyptian culture, cow's milk was associated with Hathor's maternal nurturing.
DL0043.notes: Promised Land flows with 'milk and honey' (Ex 3:8), representing divine abundance. Paul feeds Corinthians milk, not solid food (1 Cor 3:2), indicating spiritual infancy. Peter urges believers to desire pure spiritual milk (1 Pet 2:2) for growth in salvation.

DL0044.etymology: Egyptian bjt (honey, bee). From the root bjt meaning 'bee/honey'. In ancient Egypt, honey was precious as the primary sweetener, associated with divine offerings and used in mummification. Bees were considered sacred, with the pharaoh's title including 'He of the Bee'.
DL0044.notes: Symbol of the Promised Land flowing with 'milk and honey'. In biblical contexts represents divine sweetness and abundance. Egyptian medical papyri frequently mention honey for healing wounds and as preservative. Monastic texts use honey metaphorically for spiritual sweetness and divine wisdom.

DL0045.etymology: Greek σῖτος (grain, wheat, food). Borrowed during Hellenistic period when Greek agricultural terminology became standard. The word encompasses both raw grain and prepared food, reflecting the centrality of wheat in Mediterranean diet and economy.
DL0045.notes: Central to Jesus' parables of the kingdom, especially the parable of wheat and tares (Mt 13:25). Symbol of spiritual harvest and resurrection (1 Cor 15:37). Economic importance in ancient Egypt where wheat was stored in granaries and used for taxation and trade.

DL0046.etymology: Egyptian swḥ.t (egg). From the root swḥ meaning 'egg/oval object'. Eggs held profound symbolic significance in Egyptian cosmology, representing the primordial egg from which creation emerged. Associated with resurrection and new life.
DL0046.notes: Symbol of resurrection and new life in Christian contexts. Jesus uses the egg metaphor in Luke 11:12 to illustrate divine providence. Egyptian creation myths describe the cosmic egg from which the world hatched. Easter eggs in Coptic tradition symbolize Christ's resurrection from the tomb.

DL0047.etymology: Egyptian bnr (dates, sweet fruit). From the root bnr meaning 'sweet/date palm fruit'. Date palms were crucial to Egyptian agriculture, providing food, wine, and building materials. Dates represented prosperity and divine blessing in ancient Near East.
DL0047.notes: Date palms represent prosperity in Joel's prophecy (Jl 1:12). First fruits of dates brought to temple (2 Chr 31:5). Egyptian date cultivation provided staple food and export commodity. Dates stored well for travel and trade, symbolizing God's provision in desert journeys.

DL0048.etymology: Greek σάββατον (sabbath). From Hebrew שבת (shabbat) meaning 'rest/cessation'. Central to Jewish and Christian worship, representing God's rest after creation and spiritual renewal. In Coptic Christianity, became foundation of weekly liturgical cycle.
DL0048.notes: Jesus declares 'Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath' (Mk 2:27), emphasizing human welfare over ritual observance. Paul warns against judging others about sabbath observance (Col 2:16). Coptic monasticism developed daily prayer cycles based on sabbath principles of rest and worship.

DL0049.etymology: Egyptian šnj (thread, cord, rope). From the root šnj meaning 'to bind/twist together'. Thread production was fundamental to Egyptian textile industry, with elaborate spinning and weaving techniques. Threads symbolized connection, binding, and the weaving of fate.
DL0049.notes: Priestly garments bound with blue cord (Ex 28:28), representing divine connection. Jeremiah lowered into cistern with cords (Jer 38:6). Egyptian textile production required skilled thread-making for fine linen garments. Metaphorically represents human connections and divine binding.

DL0050.etymology: Egyptian sdr (wool, fleece). From the root sdr meaning 'sheep's wool/fleece'. Wool production was important in Egypt despite hot climate, used for winter garments and trade. Higher quality wool imported from cooler regions for luxury textiles.
DL0050.notes: Levitical law prohibits mixing wool and linen (Dt 22:11), representing ritual purity distinctions. Ezekiel condemns shepherds who eat sheep and wear their wool without caring for flock (Ezek 34:3). Egyptian wool trade connected Mediterranean markets with African sources.

DL0051.etymology: Egyptian ḏr.t (veil, covering). From the root ḏr meaning 'to cover/hide'. Veils served modesty, social status marking, and religious significance. Egyptian women of status wore veils in public and religious contexts for protection and dignity.
DL0051.notes: Rebekah veils herself before meeting Isaac (Gn 24:65), representing modesty and marriage preparation. Paul discusses women's head covering in worship (1 Cor 11:5), emphasizing order and respect. Moses' veil symbolizes the glory that must be unveiled in Christ (2 Cor 3:13).

DL0052.etymology: Egyptian mnḫ.t (linen garment, robe). From the root mnḫ meaning 'cloth/textile'. Fine robes indicated social status and religious office in Egyptian society. Elaborate ceremonial robes worn by priests and nobility in temple and court contexts.
DL0052.notes: Priestly robes represent sacred office and divine appointment (Ex 28:4). Jonathan gives David his robe as sign of covenant friendship (1 Sam 18:4). Father clothes prodigal son with best robe (Lk 15:22), symbolizing restoration and honor. White robes given to martyrs (Rev 6:11) represent purity and victory.

DL0053.etymology: Egyptian dbn.t (ring, circlet). From the root dbn meaning 'circular/round'. Rings served as seals of authority, symbols of covenant, and markers of social status. Egyptian jewelry displayed wealth, divine protection, and administrative power.
DL0053.notes: Pharaoh gives Joseph signet ring as symbol of delegated authority (Gn 41:42). Father puts ring on prodigal son's hand, signifying full restoration (Lk 15:22). James warns against showing partiality to those wearing gold rings (Jas 2:2), emphasizing spiritual equality over material wealth.

DL0054.etymology: Egyptian ꜥf.t (headdress, cap). From the root ꜥf meaning 'to cover head'. Head coverings indicated rank, profession, and religious status in Egyptian society. Different styles distinguished priests, officials, and social classes.
DL0054.notes: Priestly turban represents sacred office and divine calling (Ex 28:4). Joshua the high priest receives clean turban symbolizing purification from sin (Zech 3:5). Egyptian priestly caps indicated rank within temple hierarchy and access to sacred spaces.

DL0055.etymology: Greek κλείς (key). From κλείω (to close/shut). Keys symbolized authority, access, and stewardship in ancient world. Physical keys controlled access to houses, treasuries, and sacred spaces, while metaphorical keys represented spiritual authority.
DL0055.notes: Jesus gives Peter 'keys of the kingdom' (Mt 16:19), representing apostolic authority to bind and loose. Lawyers have 'taken away the key of knowledge' (Lk 11:52), preventing spiritual understanding. Christ holds 'keys of Death and Hades' (Rev 1:18), demonstrating ultimate authority over life and death.

DL0056.etymology: Greek σάνδαλον, but here meaning bed/couch rather than sandal. Egyptian beds ranged from simple reed mats to elaborate wooden frames. Beds indicated social status and provided comfort for rest, illness recovery, and social gatherings.
DL0056.notes: Paralytic carried on bed to Jesus (Mk 2:4, Lk 5:19), representing both physical infirmity and faith's determination. Jesus commands healed man to 'take up your bed and walk' (Jn 5:8), symbolizing complete restoration. Peter's shadow heals sick brought on beds (Acts 5:15), demonstrating apostolic power.

DL0057.etymology: Greek ποιμήν (shepherd). From ποίμνη (flock). Shepherding was fundamental to ancient Near Eastern life and became central metaphor for spiritual leadership. David's shepherding background prepared him for kingship. Christ as Good Shepherd represents ultimate pastoral care.
DL0057.notes: 'The Lord is my shepherd' (Ps 23:1) establishes foundational metaphor for divine care. Jesus declares 'I am the good shepherd' (Jn 10:11), contrasting with hired hands who flee danger. Paul lists pastors among spiritual gifts (Eph 4:11) for church edification. Peter exhorts elders to shepherd God's flock willingly (1 Pet 5:2).

DL0058.etymology: From Egyptian ḥmww (craftsman, skilled worker), through Demotic ḥmw, denoting one who possesses specialized skills in working with materials, particularly in construction, metalwork, or artistic creation. The term maintains its occupational designation across linguistic periods.
DL0058.notes: Technical term for skilled craftsmen, particularly those involved in temple construction and sacred arts. Biblical usage emphasizes divine inspiration of craftsmanship (Exodus 31:3). Social status varied from respected guild members to dependent workers, reflecting broader economic stratification in Coptic Egypt.

DL0059.etymology: Direct borrowing from Greek χλαμύς (chlamys), a short woolen cloak worn by soldiers and travelers, fastened at the right shoulder with a brooch. The term entered Coptic during the Hellenistic period, maintaining its original designation for outer garments of status and protection.
DL0059.notes: Appears notably in Passion narratives as the purple cloak placed on Jesus by Roman soldiers (Mark 15:17). Symbol of royal or military authority when colored purple or scarlet. Practical outer garment for protection against weather, distinguishing higher social status through quality of material and dye.

DL0060.etymology: From Greek παίζω (paizo), meaning to play, sport, or jest. Originally denoting children's games and athletic activities, the term expanded to include theatrical performance, musical entertainment, and various forms of recreation. Adopted into Coptic with similar semantic range.
DL0060.notes: Often appears in moral contexts contrasting proper spiritual discipline with worldly entertainment (1 Cor 10:7). Monastic literature frequently warns against excessive play as distraction from prayer and contemplation. However, innocent recreation and joy in God's creation receive positive treatment in pastoral writings.

BP0001.etymology: From Egyptian tp (head, top, first), through Demotic tp, denoting the uppermost part of the human body and seat of consciousness. Metaphorically extended to leadership, priority, and beginning. Central to Egyptian anatomical understanding as location of vital functions.
BP0001.notes: Fundamental anatomical term with rich metaphorical usage. Christ as 'head of the church' (Eph 1:22) establishes key theological concept. Crown of thorns on Jesus' head (Mt 27:29) emphasizes physical suffering. Hierarchical imagery in Paul's writings uses anatomical head-body relationships.

BP0002.etymology: From Egyptian jrt (eye), through Demotic bl, denoting the organ of sight and symbol of perception, knowledge, and divine oversight. Ancient Egyptian religious significance as Eye of Horus extends into Coptic theological understanding of spiritual vision and divine watchfulness.
BP0002.notes: Central to Jesus' teaching about spiritual vision: 'the eye is the lamp of the body' (Mt 6:22). Plucking out offending eye (Mk 9:47) emphasizes moral urgency. 'Every eye will see him' (Rev 1:7) in eschatological contexts. Frequently symbolizes spiritual perception and divine omniscience.

BP0003.etymology: From Egyptian jb (heart), through Demotic hy/ht, denoting both the physical organ and the seat of emotion, thought, and moral decision-making. Egyptian understanding placed the heart as center of consciousness, retained in Coptic theological anthropology as locus of spiritual life.
BP0003.notes: Most frequent term for spiritual center in Coptic Christianity. 'Create in me a clean heart' (Ps 51:10) establishes purification theme. Greatest commandment involves loving God with whole heart (Mt 22:37). Confession 'with the heart' leads to righteousness (Rom 10:9). Critical in monastic spirituality as battleground between virtue and vice.

BP0004.etymology: From Egyptian r (mouth, speech), through Demotic ry, denoting the organ of speech and consumption. Extended metaphorically to represent authority, decree, and divine word. Critical in understanding prophetic and sacramental contexts where divine word emanates.
BP0004.notes: Organ of divine revelation: man lives by every word from God's mouth (Mt 4:4). God's word from his mouth accomplishes its purpose (Is 55:11). Sharp sword proceeds from Christ's mouth (Rev 19:15). Central to sacramental theology where spoken word effects spiritual reality.

BP0005.etymology: From Egyptian msḏr (ear), through Demotic msḏ, denoting the organ of hearing and symbol of obedience and receptivity to divine instruction. Egyptian religious understanding emphasized the ear as gateway for divine commands and wisdom, retained in Coptic theological contexts.
BP0005.notes: Organ of divine communication: 'He who has ears to hear, let him hear' (Rev 2:7). Faith comes by hearing God's word (Rom 10:17). Spiritual deafness represents rejection of divine truth (Mt 13:15). Monastic tradition emphasizes listening as fundamental spiritual discipline for receiving God's guidance.

BP0006.etymology: From Egyptian fnḏ (nose), through Demotic fn, denoting the organ of smell and breathing. Associated with divine breath of life and spiritual discernment. Egyptian religious understanding connected the nose with divine inspiration and the breath of the creator god.
BP0006.notes: Central to creation narrative where God breathes life into Adam's nostrils (Gen 2:7). Hook in the nose represents divine judgment (2 Kgs 19:28). Breath of man in his nostrils emphasizes human frailty (Is 2:22). Used in medical and anatomical contexts.

BP0007.etymology: From Egyptian ns (tongue), through Demotic ls, denoting the organ of speech and taste. Extended metaphorically to represent language, confession, and praise. Critical in biblical understanding of speech as divine gift and human responsibility for words spoken.
BP0007.notes: Instrument of confession and praise: every tongue confesses Jesus as Lord (Phil 2:11). Small member with great power (Jas 3:5). Keeping tongue from evil ensures blessing (Ps 34:13, 1 Pet 3:10). Central to spiritual discipline of speech control in monastic tradition.

BP0008.etymology: From Egyptian ḏrt (hand), through Demotic tt, denoting the organ of action, creation, and blessing. Symbol of divine power and human agency. Central to understanding of divine creation through divine hands and human responsibility through manual action.
BP0008.notes: Instrument of divine action and human work. Clean hands required for worship (Ps 24:4). Offending hand to be cut off (Mt 5:30). Jesus shows hands to prove resurrection (Lk 24:39). Fearful to fall into God's hands (Heb 10:31). Central to blessing and ordination rituals.

BP0009.etymology: From Egyptian rd (foot, leg), through Demotic rt, denoting the organ of locomotion and support. Symbol of human journey, divine guidance, and spiritual pilgrimage. Essential for understanding biblical imagery of walking in God's ways.
BP0009.notes: Divine word as lamp to the feet (Ps 119:105). Light guides feet into peace (Lk 1:79). Beautiful feet of gospel messengers (Rom 10:15). Feet shod with gospel preparation (Eph 6:15). Central to pilgrimage spirituality and monastic walking meditation.

NT0001.etymology: Egyptian wꜥ.w (one, single). From the root wꜥ meaning 'one/unique/alone'. Fundamental concept in Egyptian mathematics and theology, representing unity and primacy. In Egyptian cosmology, represents the original unity before differentiation.
NT0001.notes: Central to Shema declaration 'God is one' (Dt 6:4, Mk 12:29). Mathematical and theological unity concept. Gnostic texts explore emanation from the One. Coptic Christianity emphasizes divine unity versus polytheism.

NT0002.etymology: Egyptian sn.nw (two, pair). From the root sn meaning 'two/second/brother'. Egyptian mathematics used sophisticated binary concepts. Duality fundamental in Egyptian cosmology - Upper/Lower Egypt, life/death, order/chaos.
NT0002.notes: Biblical duality themes: two masters (Mt 6:24), two witnesses (Rev 11:3), two great lights (Gn 1:16). Gnostic cosmology explores syzygy (paired opposites). Egyptian mathematics used duodecimal and binary systems.

AC0001.notes: Central concept in Gnostic and Christian philosophy, divine wisdom personified

AC0002.notes: Fundamental philosophical concept, divine truth in Gnostic thought

AC0003.notes: Central Gnostic concept of divine knowledge, esoteric wisdom

AC0004.notes: Supreme Christian virtue, divine love concept central to theology

AC0005.notes: Christian virtue of hope, eschatological expectation

ⲁⲛⲟⲕ.notes: Primary first person pronoun, most frequent word in Coptic

ⲡⲉ.notes: Essential copulative particle, most frequent grammatical word

ⲙⲛ.notes: Most frequent preposition, also used as conjunction

ⲉⲧⲃⲉ.notes: Causal preposition, very common in theological texts

ϩⲛ.notes: Primary locative preposition, second most frequent preposition

ⲉ.notes: Primary directional preposition, forms many compound verbs

ⲛ.notes: Most frequent grammatical particle, genitive marker

ϫⲉ.notes: Primary complementizer, introduces reported speech and subordinate clauses

ⲇⲉ.notes: Greek loanword, extremely common discourse connector

ⲟⲩⲛ̄.notes: Greek loanword, inferential conjunction (distinct from existential ⲟⲩⲛ)

ⲙⲉⲛ.notes: Greek loanword, emphatic and contrastive particle

ⲉϣϫⲉ.notes: Complex conditional particle, stronger than ⲉⲓ

ⲟⲩⲉⲓ.notes: Indefinite pronoun, very common in narrative

ⲗⲁⲁⲩ.notes: Universal indefinite pronoun, often in negative contexts

ⲟⲩⲁ.notes: Primary cardinal number, also indefinite article

ⲧⲉⲛⲟⲩ.notes: Primary temporal adverb for present moment

ϫⲓⲛ.notes: Primary temporal preposition for starting point

ⲇⲓⲁⲃⲟⲗⲟⲥ.notes: Greek loanword, primary term for Satan

ⲉⲩⲭⲁⲣⲓⲥⲧⲓⲁ.notes: Greek loanword, Christian sacrament and prayer

ⲁⲃⲃⲁ.notes: Aramaic loanword, monastic and biblical title

ⲡⲗⲏⲣⲱⲙⲁ.notes: Greek loanword, Gnostic and biblical concept

ⲇⲓⲕⲁⲓⲟⲥⲩⲛⲏ.notes: Greek loanword, divine and human virtue
